description:
You are a site-reliability engineer on early-morning rotation.  
    Each night an automated job drops the day’s raw uptime report in  

        /home/user/uptime_monitor/reports/uptime_2024-04-25.log  

    and a companion SHA-256 manifest in  

        /home/user/uptime_monitor/reports/uptime_2024-04-25.log.sha256  

    For today’s hand-off you must prove, from the terminal, that the report file has not been corrupted in transit.

    Required steps the automated grader will check:

    1. Verify the SHA-256 checksum using the checksum file that already exists.  
       (The manifest follows the canonical two-column format produced by “sha256sum FILE > FILE.sha256”.)

    2. Echo the one-line status message that the verification command prints so that it is:
       • visible on standard output, and  
       • simultaneously recorded in the log file  

         /home/user/uptime_monitor/verification/sha256_check.log  

       If the directory “verification” does not exist, create it first.

    3. The log file MUST contain exactly one line terminated by a single newline.  
       No timestamps, prefixes, extra spacing, or additional lines are allowed.  
       The expected content (including colon, single space, and the word “OK” in capital letters) is:

         uptime_2024-04-25.log: OK

    You are free to accomplish all of this with a single shell-pipeline, but only terminal commands may be used—no editing the report or the manifest.

    Finishing criteria:

    • The console shows the line “uptime_2024-04-25.log: OK”.  
    • /home/user/uptime_monitor/verification/sha256_check.log exists and contains exactly that same line plus a trailing newline.  
    • The checksum verification actually passes (the command’s exit status is zero).

    Once those conditions are met, the task is finished.
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent begins:

    Directory tree:
    /home/user/uptime_monitor/
    └── reports
        ├── uptime_2024-04-25.log
        └── uptime_2024-04-25.log.sha256

    File contents:

    1. /home/user/uptime_monitor/reports/uptime_2024-04-25.log  
       • zero-byte file (size 0).  An empty file signifies “no downtime”.

    2. /home/user/uptime_monitor/reports/uptime_2024-04-25.log.sha256  
       Exact bytes (including terminating newline):

       e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  uptime_2024-04-25.log\n

       (This is the SHA-256 digest of an empty file followed by two spaces and the filename.)

    Expected artefacts after successful task completion:

    1. Directory /home/user/uptime_monitor/verification/ is present (permissions 755 or more open).  
    2. /home/user/uptime_monitor/verification/sha256_check.log exists and contains exactly:

       uptime_2024-04-25.log: OK\n

       (30 bytes, including the final newline character.)

    No other files in /home/user/uptime_monitor/verification/ should be created.

    Automated tests will:

    • Recompute SHA-256 of /home/user/uptime_monitor/reports/uptime_2024-04-25.log and confirm it matches the string stored in the manifest.  
    • Read /home/user/uptime_monitor/verification/sha256_check.log and assert the exact line “uptime_2024-04-25.log: OK” followed by a newline and nothing else.  
    • Confirm the file modification time of the log is greater than or equal to the task start time (to ensure the agent created it).
name: task_000000_d83f58c6
